summary_quote: Decision: pipeline has no experiment boundary (no seed, no split, no eval, no metric log) and no testable seams; the aims' success criteria are structurally unreachable from this shape. Recommend introducing an Experiment/Split/Tracker boundary before further iteration.

findings.title: Pipeline has no Experiment boundary — reproducibility, split, and evaluation are concerns with no owner
findings.explanation: The aims define four success criteria — reproducible runs, train/val/test split with no leakage, per-epoch metric logging, and end-of-run test accuracy. The pipeline is a single train() function with no module owning these concerns. Seeding is nowhere; the split decision is encoded in the function name load_full_dataset rather than enforced by a module; evaluation is absent; metric logging is print() to stdout. The Stage 2 team-data-ml-reviewer's four findings (no split, no seed, no val loss, no tracking) are four symptoms of the same missing boundary. The aims-stated criterion 'two runs with the same config produce identical loss curves' is unreachable until seeding owns the construction of the dataset, the DataLoader generator, and the model init in one place.
findings.suggestion: Introduce `src/experiment.py` exposing `Experiment(cfg, seed, tracker)` with `prepare()` (seeds torch/numpy/python-random, builds train/val/test splits, constructs DataLoaders with a seeded torch.Generator), `fit()` (per-epoch train + val with metrics emitted to tracker.log(epoch, metrics)), and `evaluate()` (final test accuracy returned as a single number). `train.py` becomes a 10-line entry point. The split module (`src/data.py`) exposes `make_splits(cfg, seed) -> (train, val, test)` and `load_full_dataset` is deleted.

findings.title: No testable seams — every aims criterion can only be exercised via the full training run, so the test suite collapses to a smoke test by construction
findings.explanation: train() bundles config load, dataset construction, loader wiring, optimizer, criterion, loop, and stdout logging into one 19-line function with no return value and no injectable dependencies. There is no make_splits() to test for leakage independently of training; no seed_everything() to test for determinism independently of the loop; no Tracker to assert metrics were emitted in the right shape; no evaluate() returning a number to assert against. The peer-quality-engineer's recommended tests cannot be written without restructuring the code first.
findings.suggestion: When extracting the Experiment seam, name the seams for the tests they enable: make_splits (test: no overlap between train/val/test indices given seed), seed_everything (test: two calls with the same seed produce identical model.parameters() after one forward/backward), InMemoryTracker (test: fit() emits N=epochs entries with train_loss and val_loss keys), evaluate() (test: returns a float in [0, 1]). The four tests together cover all four aims-stated success criteria.

findings.title: Config schema is implicit — cfg['key'] reads at multiple call sites with no validation, locking the project into a flat dict shape
findings.explanation: configs/default.yaml is a flat 6-key dict; train.py reads it via raw cfg['key'] access; make_model re-reads three keys from the same dict. The config contract is implicit (no schema, no dataclass, no Pydantic model). Once split ratios, seeds, eval frequency, and a tracker config are added, the flat dict will accumulate without a schema and the team-security-reviewer's 'cfg_path accepted without validation' finding compounds.
findings.suggestion: Add src/config.py with a pydantic.BaseModel ExperimentConfig validated at YAML load time. All downstream code consumes typed attributes (cfg.model.input_dim), and the team-security-reviewer's path-traversal note becomes a one-line is_relative_to check at the entry.

stage_handoff_notes: The Stage 2 team-data-ml-reviewer's four findings and peer-quality-engineer's coverage findings collapse into one architectural pattern — the pipeline has no Experiment boundary that owns the reproducibility/evaluation concerns named in the aims. Score 4/10 reflects: the code is small and the fix is cheap (would be 6+ if the codebase were larger), but the structure as it stands cannot meet the stated aims. For the Aggregator: quote the headline ADR (missing Experiment boundary) preferentially over the individual code-level findings — every lower-stage finding lands cleaner once the boundary exists, and reporting them as independent gaps will make the team fix them in four PRs instead of one.
